item.content:encoded: June only has 30 days…

item.title: Minor Edit
item.content:encoded:
I am both astonished and slightly disappointed that nobody noticed the wrong german in this one.

Instead of "Fantastische!" it must read just "Fantastisch!". (The e is only applicable when declension comes into play.)

EDIT: I made the one-letter change. If this was anyhow against policy, i deeply apologize and hope staff can correct this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I want to upvote, but… this tugging at pity won't get my upvote every time. Neutral vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh god that's the horrible kind of tragic bizarre that brought me to this site in the first place. Good but… dear god please tell me the flawed one gets a mercy killing or something. SOMETHING so I don't feel like I'm watching that episode of Futurama again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Instances of SCP-2994 are inactive until a human writes on them (…) which causes them to display simple incomplete phrases in German.

Sample texts manifested by SCP-2994-28 during containment. Grammar and spelling have been unaltered.

i dont know what i did rong

So it can be assumed that the phrases have been translated from German. In which case, how can a spelling mistake ("rong") be translated from another language?

I still like the concept, so upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Heh, not my thing. Seems to be trying too hard to tug at the reader's heartstrings. SCP-2994-28 randomly writing in English when every other instance writes in German also comes off as a bad excuse to have the text not only be in English, but also misspelled for extra "aww".

And how did the school not notice until parents mentioned it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm sorry, but the second addendum is an incredibly large letdown, and forced. Fix that and it'll be perfect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
dear god what

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was hovering around no-vote until I got to the messages from -28, at which point I could not scroll up and down vote hard enough. Very cliche ending. Suicidal white.board, while perhaps being your starting point, is the weakest part of this entry, to me.

item.title: I'm sorry
item.content:encoded: I think the implication is that is the speaking of the TEACHERS dog!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Please please someone tell me that log at the end isn't one of the dogs talking…anything but that… :'(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed. The ending just felt tacked on, and ruined it for me.

item.title: Great Job
item.content:encoded: I like this skip. Here, have an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Too bad, I liked it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All right, so from what I can see here, the two overwhelming complaints are that everything relating to -28 is cheap and hamfisted and that the teacher comes off way too mad-sciency. As a result, I'm going to work on a re-rewrite to address these criticisms. Thanks to everyone who voted and commented.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As someone who in a hipsterish manner can claim he did stuff like this before it was popular (though not in a nice manner like this - I just have references to numerous numbered documents and guidelines of the likes of As material produced during the experiment appears to lack anomalous properties, its use is approved for non-critical applications as defined in document CSG-0371-N., I disagree.

Sure, it can be used as you say, which is usually stupid (as you say) but really, what referring to nonexistent documents does and should do is allow you not to write about things that would derail the article you're trying to write, but still retain realism by the act of implying that the information is readily available to whoever is reading the document in-universe (as in the above case would be usage guidelines for non-anomalous materials originating from unrelated anomalous processes).

Really, unlike expungements which can be a fairly powerful tool in terms of, well, eliciting response from a reader, this is just a bit of window dressing that doesn't need much attention. In Kate's articles, I outright liked this method as it was fresh and new, never mind the article names were fairly amusing (which is always a plus), but really we're giving the feature more attention than it deserves.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like to think that the teacher was not human. Maybe that's just how education works in her dimension.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What I get out of them is the opportunity to imagine what such a book would contain. They flesh out the Foundation for me and really allow me to suspend my disbelief. I find they "dry out" the tone as well. Here's an SCP on a living whiteboard and instead of being shocked and appalled, the researcher writing the report is making sure the punctuation of his reference is up to proper standards.

But of course, to each their own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm honestly tired of SCPs where the maker's identity is known but they were somehow able to completely drop off the map on a moment's notice before the Foundation got to their house and could not be found.

Also, I have a hard time believing that a woman cares about her students that much while caring about her own dog so little. Especially when I'm pretty sure there are computer programs that could do this exact job with zero animal cruelty.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It just seems utterly pointless to me; it gives absolutely no new information, while at the same time implying that there is additional information to it that somehow we should be privy to, but it's "above our pay grade" in the sense that it doesn't exist

To me it just seems like a snarky way to bring back random DATA EXPUNGED's without actually technically hiding anything

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I find this little better than the original. Kalinin is right that the explanation borders on "mad scientist" parody - "Kids aren't doing well in German? Quick, lemme make some talking whiteboards out of several stolen dogs."

Besides, I feel like I have seen this sort of a theme, an organism mimicking something else, with Accipiter sopwithii, and that one was thirty kinds of charming in ways that this one isn't - you really got a feel that it was a captive animal, just in the shape of an airplane.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure what to think about them - here they *really* give out some sort of "adopting features from successful articles without rhyme or reason" vibe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tone is solid, concept is vastly improved from the original, and I'd be all geared to upvote if not for the ending. It didn't really add any sort of mystery or terror to the skip, and came across as hamfisted and trite. I'd recommend removing it. I'd much rather have a skip with a decently interesting concept and nothing else than a skip with said concept soiled by a (in my opinion) failed attempt to give it a backstory and emotional appeal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think they're brilliant. They're my favorite new trend since we changed amnesiacs to amnestics. I think it adds such an air of believability to an article. I mean, that's what footnotes are actually for. Furthermore, of course a bunch of researchers are going to be publishing research on all of the anomalous things they encounter. Each team wouldn't be an island, and researchers into new SCPs would most definitely utilize old research and would definitely be expected to cite their sources.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I remember the old one. I despised it. This is a vast improvement. The tone is very well done and you took what I thought to be a terrible concept and made it into something both believable and interesting. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh, the end is really the only way I could think of to bring across the "suicidal whiteboard" thing, which was the main theme. I'm not sure how to do it otherwise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This version hasn't significantly improved over the last version that I read in chat, but I'd still novote on it. My main issue remains the closing to the article, which still feels rather cliche to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed, thank you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it's an improvement over the old one (granted, I don't really remember the old one). Both instances of "ago" in the second paragraph of the first addendum should be "prior" or "previous", though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What the hell.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly, I didn't really feel like I could create much mystery without not actually providing enough at all. The second point, I will concede, though. I'll add something to fix that. Thanks for reviewing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh, honestly, I started off with the suicidal whiteboard, and built everything around that. But aside from that, I'm not sure how I can fix it. If it goes under 0, I'll take it down and rework it again. Thanks for the review!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really do not like those bibliography style footnotes

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The "suffering" whiteboard dialogue is tacked-on and an aggresively cheap feels swipe. This entry exemplifies one of my least favorite burgeoning tropes: a slightly interesting concept that replaces actual development, narrative, and storytelling with shoehorned child/animal suffering to cover its lack of depth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think I actually prefer the previous version. I don't particularly care for the notion that some random German teacher was able to cobble these together out of dogs; it takes away from being able to contemplate what kind of twisted person would go to the trouble of making responsive whiteboards that feel pain. "Ms. So-and-so because she wanted teaching aids for her German class" is not really a satisfactory answer to that mystery for me.

Also, does this really need to be a "bigger on the inside" style spatial anomaly? That just feels kind of unnecessary. I can just imagine the German teacher at her house, merrily constructing these, and thinking to herself, "hmm, I can't quite fit everything in here, I can either make the casing a little bigger, or BEND THE LAWS OF SPACE-TIME ITSELF". C'mon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey there! This is my first SCP, a rewrite of Jekeled's 2994.

Thanks to everyone who reviewed this in chat, as well as Azzleflux, Trolley33, Tagliafierro, Cimmerian, Scorpion451, WalrusKing, Fiestasheep, MrKarma and probably a few others I can't remember right now (poke me if I forgot you, sorry) for help with ideas, formatting, spelling and grammar, KateMcTiriss for the footnote, Riemann, Jekeled and djkaktus for allowing me to rewrite and special super uber thanks to MorgiePie and InsipidParoxysm for their science help and Vivax for his thorough deconstruction of this.
